planting.note: Plant corms 4 inches deep in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; night-scented gladiolus grows in winter and spring (following the Cape winter-rainfall cycle), producing its pale, cream-ivory flowers in late winter and spring with a powerful evening fragrance. Unlike most garden gladioli, this species grows from autumn through spring and goes summer dormant. Native to wet marshes and seeps of the Cape coastal zone; it tolerates seasonally moist to waterlogged conditions better than other gladioli.

directions: Plant corms in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; night-scented gladiolus is one of the most fragrant of all gladiolus species — producing in late winter and spring slender, elegant flower spikes bearing 6–10 pale cream-ivory, creamy-yellow, or very pale greenish-white flowers of delicate, almost translucent quality that in the daytime appear quietly beautiful but subdued, and in the evening fill the surrounding air with an extraordinarily sweet, powerfully rich, vanilla-jasmine-like fragrance that far surpasses any hybrid gladiolus in scent intensity. This powerful evening fragrance — evolved to attract night-flying hawk moths — is the species' defining feature and the reason for both the common name (night-scented) and the species epithet 'tristis' (sad, mournful — referring perhaps to the pale, ghostly flowers that show their best quality only after dark when they become intensely fragrant). Native to the moist coastal seeps and wetland margins of the Western Cape, it is unusual among gladioli in tolerating and even preferring seasonally wet conditions, making it valuable for moist borders and rain garden sites in zones 8–11. A plant to site near a window or garden seating area where the evening fragrance can be fully appreciated.

planting.note: Plant corms 4 inches deep in moist to well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; butterfly gladiolus is a summer-rainfall species from the Drakensberg foothills and eastern South Africa that tolerates summer moisture well, unlike the Cape winter-rainfall gladioli. It spreads aggressively by stolons in favorable conditions. Flowers July–September (Northern Hemisphere). The unusual grayish-green-yellow-and-purple bicolor flower is unlike any other gladiolus and has been used extensively in breeding for unusual color forms.

directions: Plant corms in moist to well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; butterfly gladiolus is the most distinctively unusual-colored of the commonly available species gladioli — producing in late summer (July–September) tall spikes of flowers in a complex, subtle, difficult-to-describe combination of grayish-green to yellowish-green outer coloring overlaid and mottled with purple-violet inside — the whole flower an intriguing, unusual, somewhat somber mixture of muddy green-yellow and purple that is unlike any other color in the gladiolus genus and perfectly captures the muted, mysterious 'butterfly' quality that inspired the common name. The flowers are substantial and well-formed, the spikes tall and elegant, and the overall effect in a planting scheme is a striking departure from the clear, saturated colors of most gladioli into the unusual territory of complex, multi-toned, gray-green-and-purple harmony. It spreads aggressively by underground stolons, building large colonies in suitable moist, fertile conditions — a vigorous and persistent perennial rather than a tender annual-treated corm. The interesting color has made it valuable in gladiolus breeding for unusual, bicolor, and broken-color forms. Outstanding for sophisticated color-aware planting schemes in zones 7–10.

subcategory: deep blue spectacular South African Cape corm

planting.note: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy soil in full sun; splendid wine cups follows the Cape winter-rainfall cycle — autumn emergence, winter-spring bloom, complete summer dormancy. Keep bone dry in summer. The species name 'splendidissima' (most splendid) is justified: the flowers are among the most intense, saturated, pure deep blue of any plant in the world. Best in containers in areas with wet summers to control summer moisture.

directions: Plant corms in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy soil in full sun; splendid wine cups — the botanical superlative in the species epithet 'splendidissima' (most splendid) is fully earned — is one of the most intense, saturated, deep-blue flowering plants in existence, producing in late winter and spring wide-open, cup-shaped flowers of extraordinary, gemlike, deep ultramarine-blue to dark royal-blue that is so saturated and vivid it appears almost luminescent in sunlight, with a small, contrasting white or very pale center zone at the base of each petal that intensifies the optical power of the surrounding dark blue. Unlike the bicolor G. radians (blue with red center blotches), G. splendidissima achieves its beauty through the purity and depth of a single, extraordinary, supremely saturated blue — arguably the deepest, most intense, most unmistakably magnificent pure blue of any spring-flowering plant regularly available to gardeners. Native to a restricted area of the coastal Western Cape fynbos where it grows in sandy, peaty, seasonally moist soils on the coastal flats. A collector's prize — not widely available but obtainable from specialist Cape bulb suppliers — of singular botanical distinction for the quality of its flower color alone.

subcategory: late-summer South African pink iris corm

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; pink tritonia blooms in late summer (July–September) on upright stems with flowers arranged alternately in two rows (disticha = two-rowed) along the upper stem. Unlike the Cape winter-rainfall tritonias, this species is summer-growing, tolerating summer moisture well. It can naturalize in mild-winter, well-drained garden conditions and spreads by corm offsets.

directions: Plant corms in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; pink tritonia is a late-summer blooming South African iris-family plant from the summer-rainfall areas of the Drakensberg — producing in July through September upright, slender stems bearing alternating rows of medium-sized, open-faced, clear to deep pink flowers with slightly darker veining, each flower funnel-shaped with reflexing petals, arranged in two precise rows along the upper stem (the botanical meaning of 'disticha'). The flowers are produced successively along the stem over a 4–6 week period, providing extended late-summer color in an individually distinct and refined flower form. The clear pink color, clean and uncomplex, is particularly valuable in the late-summer border when pink is difficult to source in iris-family plants. It is a summer-rainfall species tolerating summer moisture well, making it more practical for the Gulf Coast and humid-summer gardens of zones 7–10 than the more demanding Cape winter-rainfall iris species. It spreads gradually by corm offsets to form larger clumps, and naturalizes modestly in favorable well-drained, sunny garden conditions over time.

planting.note: Plant corms 4 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pillans's watsonia is evergreen (unlike many other watsonia species) and blooms in autumn (February–April in South Africa; August–October in the Northern Hemisphere) rather than spring. This autumn bloom timing, combined with the evergreen habit, makes it distinctive among the watsonias. It is a robust species that spreads by corm offsets and self-seeds in favorable conditions.

directions: Plant corms in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pillans's watsonia stands apart from the commonly grown, spring-blooming pink watsonia (W. borbonica) by two characteristics: it blooms in autumn (August–October in the Northern Hemisphere) rather than spring, and the flowers are a warm orange to orange-red to salmon-orange rather than the pink to magenta of the spring-blooming species — the warm-toned tubular gladiolus-like flowers on the tall, upright, spike-like stems standing out distinctly in the autumn border when the warm orange-red of watsonia is available at the same time as autumn perennials in yellow and bronze. The plant is also evergreen, maintaining its broad, iris-like, sword-shaped fans of deep-green leaves through winter. The autumn bloom timing fills a gap in the South African iris-family plant calendar in the garden — providing the characteristic watsonia spike-form in the autumn season when no other watsonia species is in bloom. Combined with autumn-blooming agapanthus, late kniphofias, and autumn grasses, it provides a warm, orange-accented, late-season contribution to the South African plant garden.
